one_line_takeaway: Applying a purpose-built cross-language taint analyzer (MICRYSCOPE) to 9,403 real-world MCP servers, the authors find that 19.7% of the 720 crypto-using servers misuse cryptography (missing authentication/AEAD, weak hashes, hardcoded keys, ECB mode, etc.), with misuse concentrated in Python code, Developer Tools/Data Science categories, and the Smithery Registry market.
key_finding: Of 9,403 MCP servers analyzed, 720 contained cryptographic logic, and of those, 142 (19.7%) exhibited a cryptographic misuse per the 8 rules. Misuse is unevenly distributed: by market, Smithery Registry had the highest misuse share (42% of flagged servers) despite being smaller than Mcpmarket, which is the largest registry (3,196 servers vs. Smithery's 2,538) but had a lower misuse share (37%); Pulse MCP (1,999 servers) accounted for 10% of misuses; Cursor Directory, Modelcontextprotocol.io, and Awesome MCP contributed smaller shares (with Awesome MCP at 1%). By language, Python showed a disproportionately higher misuse density than JavaScript despite JavaScript having a larger crypto-using population in most markets (e.g., in Mcpmarket: Python 89 crypto-enabled servers with 30 misuses vs. JavaScript 112 crypto-enabled servers with 17 misuses). By functional category, Developer Tools and Data Science & ML together accounted for more than half of all observed misuses, while categories like Learning & Documentation, Social Media Management, and Mobile Development had minimal misuse counts. By rule, R7 (Missing Integrity Protection, e.g., AES-CBC/CTR without MAC/GCM) was the single most prevalent violation, followed by R3 (Weak Hash Functions, MD5/SHA-1) and R5 (Static PRNG Seeds); multi-rule co-occurrence was common, with R3+R7 and R6+R7 (insecure block mode plus missing authentication) being the most frequent combinations (24 and 8 servers respectively per Figure 13b, against 81 single-rule R7 cases as the largest single bucket).
core_contribution: The paper presents MICRYSCOPE, the first domain-specific static-analysis framework for detecting cryptographic API misuse specifically in Model Context Protocol (MCP) server implementations. MICRYSCOPE has three components: (1) an AST-guided cross-language intermediate representation (IR) that normalizes cryptographic API calls (name, parameters, produced variables, dependencies) into a language-agnostic JSON representation across the ~10+ languages MCP servers are written in; (2) a hybrid dependency analyzer that builds a graph combining deterministic 'must' edges (from interprocedural Def-Use analysis) with conservative 'may' edges (from resource fingerprinting and API semantic categorization), the latter specifically modeling the fact that MCP tools are weakly-coupled functions that an LLM composes at runtime rather than through explicit static call chains; and (3) a taint-based misuse detector that propagates taint from sources (user input, config, env vars) to sinks (crypto calls, file writes, network sends) and checks propagation chains against 8 hand-crafted misuse rules (R1-R8, e.g., fixed keys, fixed IV/salt, weak hashes, insecure KDF config, static PRNG seed, ECB mode, missing integrity protection, deprecated algorithms/APIs). The authors then run MICRYSCOPE over 9,403 MCP servers scraped from 7 registries (Mcpmarket, Smithery Registry, Pulse MCP, Cursor Directory, Modelcontextprotocol.io, Awesome MCP, Glama AI) to produce what they call the first ecosystem-wide empirical study of cryptographic misuse in MCP, including three case studies of real vulnerabilities.
threat_model: Not an attacker-vs-defender adversarial threat model in the traditional sense; this is a measurement/detection paper. The implicit risk model is: MCP servers act as trusted gateways to sensitive data/services, but MCP's protocol-level guarantees (JSON schema validation, capability declaration, request ID traceability) provide no authenticity or confidentiality, so developers bolt on ad hoc cryptography that is prone to well-known misuse classes (hardcoded keys, ECB mode, MD5, fixed IVs, missing MAC/AEAD, weak PRNG seeding, weak KDF params, deprecated primitives). A secondary, MCP-specific risk highlighted is that because MCP tools are weakly coupled and orchestrated by an LLM at runtime rather than through explicit code paths, an LLM can compose two individually 'reasonable' functions (e.g., a naive password-truncation key-derivation function plus a correct AES-CBC encryption function) into an insecure composition that would not appear as an edge in a conventional static control-flow graph. The paper's case studies show concrete consequences: hardcoded/leaked LLM API keys (e.g., Gemini keys in Excel Master MCP Server and dify-for-dsl MCP Server) enabling billing abuse; a 'Crypto MCP Server' exposing a DES/ECB-mode encryption tool as a supply-chain risk to downstream consumers; and the 1Panel MCP Server computing an auth token as MD5("1panel"+apiKey+timestamp), enabling offline brute-force/token forgery to bypass a deployment pipeline.
limitations: The authors themselves state three limitations: (1) the analysis is purely static (IR construction + taint propagation), so cryptographic parameters generated dynamically at runtime (e.g., IVs from system time or environment randomness) may remain unresolved symbolic variables rather than detected insecure constants, causing false negatives; (2) the 8-rule detection set is bounded by predefined patterns, so misuse hidden behind custom wrapper functions or indirect/obfuscated implementations can evade detection — illustrated by a documented false negative in the MongoDB_Atlas server where a custom md5() wrapper concatenating API key, realm, and nonce into a Digest-auth-style scheme was missed because the sensitive values were obscured through string concatenation before hashing; (3) MICRYSCOPE analyzes code-level misuse only and does not cover deployment-level issues (insecure transport, weak/outdated dependencies), which the authors flag as future work. Separately (not stated as a limitation by the authors but evident from the reported numbers), the manual validation sample for the false-negative rate was only 100 servers (5 found to be false negatives), and the 0% false-positive claim covers only the 142 servers actually flagged — the study does not report inter-rater reliability or an independent second annotator for the manual validation.

datasets_used: 9,403 MCP servers crawled from GitHub API and 7 registries: Mcpmarket, Smithery Registry, Pulse MCP, Cursor Directory, Modelcontextprotocol.io, Awesome MCP, Glama AI
quantitative_results: 720 of 9,403 MCP servers (7.7%) contain cryptographic logic
142 of 720 crypto-enabled servers (19.7%) exhibit cryptographic misuse
Manual validation: 5% false-negative rate (5/100 sampled misuse-free servers actually had misuse); 0% false-positive rate (142/142 flagged servers confirmed as true misuses)
Smithery Registry: 42% misuse share among flagged servers (2,538 total servers)
Mcpmarket: 37% misuse share (3,196 total servers, the largest registry)
Pulse MCP: 10% misuse share (1,999 total servers)
Python misuse rate cited as 34% in the abstract; per Table 3, Mcpmarket example: Python 89 crypto servers / 30 misuses vs. JavaScript 112 crypto servers / 17 misuses
Developer Tools + Data Science & ML categories together account for >50% of all observed misuses
R7 (Missing Integrity Protection) is the single most prevalent rule violation (81 servers with only R7 triggered, per Fig. 13b)
R3+R7 co-occurrence: 24 servers; R6+R7 co-occurrence: 8 servers (next most common multi-rule combinations)
Analysis pipeline: ~6,000 lines of Python 3.11; full 9,403-server corpus processed in under ~45 minutes total on a single workstation (Intel Core Ultra 7 258V, 32GB RAM)
baselines_compared: No prior automated tool is directly benchmarked against MICRYSCOPE; the paper positions itself as the first MCP-specific cryptographic misuse detector and instead validates itself via manual ground-truth labeling of a sampled subset (100 servers for false-negative check, all 142 flagged servers for false-positive check). Related general-purpose/other-domain crypto misuse detectors (e.g., CryptoGo, Crylogger, CryptoEval, Cryptody, Gopher) are discussed only in Related Work as prior art, not run head-to-head on this dataset.
